text: The surgeon prepares to perform a laparoscopic myomectomy, aiming to remove three fibroids: a small 1x1 cm fibroid, a medium 5x5 cm fundal fibroid, and a large 12x12 cm myoma located partly on the stomach and partly on the cervical area of the anterior surface.

The surgeon uses four laparoscopic ports for the procedure, injecting diluted vasopressin into the fibroid stalk to minimize blood loss and maintain a clear surgical field. A bipolar cutter is employed to address the subserosal fibroid, while a harmonic device is planned for the intramural fibroid. The subserosal fibroid is small, with visible circumferential vessels and no major vessels in the stalk.

The surgeon removes the anterior cervical and isthmic fibroid, which extends into the broad ligament. The fibroid is positioned below the round ligament, with half of the round ligament visible above it.

The surgeon separates the bladder peritoneum, which detaches easily, revealing a thin capsule. The myoma and endometrial junction are clearly visible, with the myoma partially embedded in the myometrium and extending into the serosa. The remaining vasopressin is injected into the area.

The surgeon performs hydro dissection in the area, causing a bulging distal to the puncture site with visible blanching, turning the tissue to a marble white appearance. A transverse elliptical incision is then made.

The surgeon makes an incision in the center of the myoma, with the larger portion located on the lower side. The incision is directed slightly upward to avoid the ureter on the lateral edges. Vasopressin is visible emerging from between the capsule and the fibroid, which is covered by a thin capsule.

The surgeon identifies a proper plane for the myomectomy, facilitating easier dissection of the fibroid. The fibroid is removed without using a morcellator, instead utilizing an endo bag for extraction. An incision is extended to approximately three centimeters to accommodate the removal of the fibroid tissue.

The surgeon inserts a myoma screw into the deeply located myoma, with only 20-30% visible. Using a peeling technique, traction is applied to both edges in opposite directions for separation. A ball grasper and harmonic device are used to achieve a bloodless separation.

The surgeon adjusts the incision to be slightly larger, facilitating easier removal of the fibroid during the myomectomy. The fibroid is gradually being extracted, with separation occurring first on one side and then the other. The myoma screw position is frequently changed to optimize traction and counter-traction. Tubal patency is confirmed visually, indicating both tubes are patent.

After 20-25 minutes, some bleeding is observed as the vasopressin effect diminishes. The surgeon adjusts the position of the myoma, applying significant traction to facilitate its inoculation. A barb suture is prepared for closing the myomectomy incision, ensuring proper approximation of the incision margins.

The surgeon identifies the glistening white fibroid, indicating the proper plane of dissection for enucleation. The fibroid is gradually being separated using a harmonic instrument, with slight oozing observed as the vasopressin effect diminishes. No significant bleeding is encountered during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies two fibroids, one larger on the anterior aspect and a smaller one on the posterior surface. The fibroids are gradually enucleated, with visible traction applied to aid in their removal. The fibroid on the right side is slowly emerging, with approximately five percent of it visible as the enucleation progresses.

The surgeon focuses on the basal part of the fibroid, where the feeders are located, using traction and counter-traction techniques with a myoma screw to aid in enucleation. The fibroid is gradually extracted, revealing a small stalk remaining. The uterine cavity remains intact, as a uterine sound manipulator was used, ensuring the endometrium was not entered. A barb suture is used for closing the myoma bed in two layers.

The surgeon observes small bleeders as the effect of vasopressin diminishes. The myometrium is being cut, and the fibroid has been removed. A barb suture is used to close the incision, with the needle introduced through a 5mm port. Full-thickness bites are taken through the muscle layer, securing both the upper and lower margins for optimal suture strength and tension.

The surgeon completes the myomectomy by cutting the fibroids into small pieces using scissors. The fibroid tissue is removed through a three-centimeter incision, facilitated by a ten-millimeter secondary port. The suture margins appear well-aligned, and the urine is clear.
